title: Sonography training - Does practice make perfect?
description: *An adapted version of this post was originally provided to first-year medical ultrasound students at QUT to encourage discussion on ways they could accelerate their learning.*
contents:
Ultrasound examinations are unique and rely heavily on the operator’s
skill. In contrast, during Computed Tomography (CT) and Magnetic
Resonance Imaging (MRI) examinations, image quality is not directly
coupled to the operator’s physical dexterity and real-time cognitive
processing speed. General Radiography still relies heavily on the
ability of the Radiographer to position a patient correctly. However,
the skills to precisely set exposure factors depend less on the
Radiographer with the more widespread use of computerised and digital
radiography. The distinctive machine-human interface required during an
ultrasound exam is necessary and directly influences each exam’s success
or failure.
Sonography requires the operator’s integrated skill to manipulate the
transducer in various planes to produce a diagnostic image whilst
adjusting controls that are not automatically adjusted by the machine.
All the while simultaneously interpreting many images per second to
determine if any significant pathology is present. The diagnostic
ability of an ultrasound scan remains in real-time and at the point of
care. Indeed, if a significant pathology is present and not imaged
appropriately, it will not be detected in a review by the reporting
specialist. This combination of abilities highlights the high level of
skills required of a Sonographer. Sonography, in many ways, is like
other activities that require a synergy of skills to perform well, such
as playing an instrument. A high level of coordination and knowledge of
music and how notes and sounds fit well together are required. Or like
many sporting activities, which require superior fitness coupled with
specific skills and knowledge of the game. Therefore, the training of a
Sonographer takes many years.
Like other health disciples, student sonographers are exposed to
various training environments; at times, these are effective and provide
ample opportunity to learn and develop. At other times learning can be
complex due to external factors.
At this point I proposed some questions and introduced other
information and research in the field of skill acquisition.
Some readers may be familiar with the terms purposeful or Deliberate
Practice (DP). Which has been popularised in several books, some of
which are best sellers. The book Outliers: The story of success
by Malcolm Gladwell (Gladwell 2008)
has several elements, but a vital tenet of the book argues the secret to
success is the notion of 10,000 hours of practice. Highlighting that a
significant degree of time focused on a task is required to become truly
an expert in any field, more so than any innate natural ability or
genetic predisposition. Gladwell writes on many levels giving examples,
some personal and others from popular culture such as The Beatles and
Bill Gates, and discussing the importance of environment and
socio-economic factors being highly correlated to success.
So, a significant amount of time is required, but for many of us,
this is predetermined. Such as the days a week we work or are rostered
in ultrasound, and some students do more than others. Other authors have
stated that there is truth to the 10,000 hours rule, but this is perhaps
a little simplistic. Another interesting book is a book by Matthew Syed
called Bounce (Syed and Clamp
2010). Matthew Syed is a journalist who writes for the Times
in the UK and lectures on many topics about success and high
performance; interestingly, he became a world-class table tennis player,
achieving fantastic success in this discipline. His book and others like
it state that practice is essential, but also practice must be
purposeful; otherwise, it is useless. At least sometimes, training must
force us out of our comfort zone to “overload” the current systems to
cause improvements. Also, he stresses the importance of feedback as
necessary for progress, as this helps us correct and fine-tune our
abilities.
The above authors have likely drawn much of their influence from the
work of Anders
Ericsson, a professor of Psychology at Floria State University. He
has spent his life researching human performance. Recenly his work has
investigated the health care field, particularly the skill and expertise
of doctors and nurses. His research and others in this field focused on
the relative expertise and abilities as doctors progress in their
careers. He argued the skills of doctors (at least those working in the
clinical and diagnostic areas) could even worsen as time progresses. He
explains this as a combination of factors, such as the inability to
receive feedback on decisions made. Health care is complicated, and it
is often difficult to define which intervention has been effective. In
health, there is also the assumption that once you have finished your
training and gained the knowledge, you are suitably skilled to perform
at a high level. Anders argues that this is far from the truth and
discusses the adage “see one, do one, teach one” and how this
institutional thinking is damaging.
Health care workers, after a certain point in time, become
comfortable with their abilities and, therefore, without knowing it,
fail to progress further even if their knowledge advances. Anders argues
that the key to the continued improvement of skills will only occur when
a few critical components of learning and practice are adhered to. One
crucial element is feedback; Anders with Robert Pool, in their book Peak
(Ericsson and Pool
2016) explain that some doctors will improve over time but
only when they can get direct feedback. He uses the model of surgeons
that perform prostatectomies due to the presence of prostate cancer. For
the surgery to be effective, the entire prostate must be removed with
histologically clear margins when the specimen is sent for analysis.
Successful surgery directly impacts the patient’s long-term survival
rate, and the doctor will receive the results of their surgery a short
time after they have performed it. It has become clear that those
surgeons who have performed this operation many more times have much
higher patient survival rates. In comparison, those doctors who have
performed far fewer surgeries are almost always worse. The more
experienced surgeons have practised more, gained feedback, and then
adapted their technique to better outcomes. This adaptation, in part, is
a product of the learner developing some novel way to solve a complex
issue. Anders describes this as “mental representations”, and there is
much to this described in his book. However, this youtube video also
explains it quite well.
YouTube Video -
How to Master Anything: PEAK by Anders Ericsson | Core Message
The core components mentioned in the book (and summary video) are
having specific, attainable goals, focusing on the problem at hand,
gaining feedback and practising at least part of the time right at the
limit of our abilities. Added to this is an effective coach to ensure
the best chance to improve. Importantly, improvement occurs much quicker
when these elements are present than simply spending time “just
doing.”
So how does any of this apply to Sonography? At this point, I
asked the students to consider some of the above topics and provide
insights from their own lived experiences.
To get the ball rolling, I stated feedback is essential. For many
students, feedback, particularly on areas of technique, is likely
regularly provided early in training. However, this type of direct
feedback becomes less common as the basic skills and knowledge of probe
manipulation and scan technique are understood. So, are there other ways
students can continue to gather feedback in busy clinical
environments?
Anders also points out how the health care industry is excellent at
promoting and providing opportunities for continuing professional
development (CPD). There are endless opportunities to attend seminars
and lectures on various discipline-specific areas. Still, he argues most
of these are useless and don’t improve the skills of those that attend.
Remember, knowledge does not equate to a new skill. On the contrary,
workshops are immensely effective, where a student can gain hands-on
training whilst being instructed by an expert in a particular area. Food
for thought, perhaps we should preference the available hands-on
workshops at conferences rather than bothering with all the lectures?
Something I think the Sonography profession already does well and
provides a good balance.
What about mental representations? Are there ways students
can develop these? Anders offers some potential insights on this, and
the example in the book (and video above), about his students who were
tasked with remembering number sequences, is a good one. He had an
accomplished student Steve explain to another student Dario his
technique of remembering the numbers like a tree branch, which
subsequently allowed Dario to improve rapidly and even surpass Steve’s
abilities to recall the numbers. Anders also uses the example of
training schemes with doctors where during complex operations, he had
expert doctors explain what they were thinking during the procedure by
asking specific questions. This process teased out those mental
representations that may be subconscious for many experts but made a
massive difference in student learning. For other ideas related to
ultrasound, my colleagues and I wrote an article on Deliberate practice
in Sonography a few years ago (Edwards, Chamunyonga, and Clarke
2018).
In summary, the task was for students to reflect on their own
experiences and consider ways they could take greater responsibility to
enhance their learning outcomes. The ideas and background in Deliberate
Practice provided a valuable springboard and produced a range of good
ideas and insights from the student, which they generously shared with
their peers. Please feel free to use and adapt for your students in
ultrasound or other professions.



Edwards, Christopher, Crispen Chamunyonga, and Jillian Clarke. 2018.
“The role of deliberate practice in
development of essential sonography skills.”
Sonography 5 (2): 76–81. https://doi.org/10.1002/sono.12146.


Ericsson, Anders, and Robert Pool. 2016. Peak:
Secrets from the new science of expertise. Random House.


Gladwell, Malcolm. 2008. Outliers: The story of
success. Little, Brown.


Syed, Matthew, and James Clamp. 2010. Bounce.
HarperCollins London.
